summary:
The First Transcontinental Railroad, completed on 10 May 1869 at Promontory Summit, Utah, was an engineering marvel that linked the eastern United States to the Pacific Coast with approximately 3,000 kilometers of continuous railway. It was one of the signature infrastructure achievements of the nineteenth century, transforming the American West, accelerating industrial development, and embodying both the ambitions and the contradictions of the era.

Construction was divided between two companies: the Union Pacific built westward from Omaha, Nebraska, and the Central Pacific built eastward from Sacramento, California. The Central Pacific's workforce was predominantly Chinese immigrants — some 10,000 to 20,000 men who performed the most difficult and dangerous work, including the blasting through the Sierra Nevada mountains. The Union Pacific relied heavily on Irish immigrants and Civil War veterans. Both workforces labored under brutal conditions for low wages.

The railroad's completion in 1869 reduced the transcontinental journey from months to about a week. Freight costs dropped dramatically. The cattle trade of the Great Plains, the mining industries of Nevada and California, and the agricultural development of the interior West all depended on the railroad's reach. Eastern manufactured goods flowed west while raw materials moved east, knitting a national economy together.

The railroad's construction and operation also had devastating consequences for the Native American peoples of the Great Plains. Buffalo herds were disrupted, then systematically destroyed to starve the Plains nations into submission. Military campaigns used the railroad to deploy troops rapidly, contributing to the dispossession and confinement of nations that had shaped the American West for millennia. The railroad was thus simultaneously a triumph of American industrial enterprise and a tool of the dispossession that the [[event:2564|Trail of Tears]] and later conflicts had begun.
